title: Friday Sermon 17 May 2002
description:
Today the sermon shall be on the Divine attribute of al-Waḥab (The Bestower).

Waḥab denotes a Being Who bestows on everyone according to his or her rights and Who showers blessings on people on a wide scale and that this bestowal is free from any motive or sense of recompense.*

Several verses of The Holy Quran, The Ahadith and various writings further elucidate the subject:

The Holy Prophet (peace and blessings be on him) said that if Allah so wished He could turn the heart of anyone among mankind and that the Holy Prophet (peace and blessings be on him) would constantly pray to Allah the Exalted to keep his heart firm in matters of faith.

(Abu Isa Muhammad ibn Isa al-Tirmidhi.  'Jami al-Tirmidhi').

The Holy Prophet (peace and blessings be on him) recounted that Allah the Exalted had bestowed Lailat al-Qadr (Night of the Decree) on his people, a blessing hitherto not bestowed on any other people.

(Ali ibn Abdul Malik of India.  'Kanz al-Ummāl fī Sunan al-Aqwāl wal afāl').

Several of the Quranic verses pertain to the Abrahamic prayers and prayers made by other prophets of God, including the Prophets Zachariah, David, Solomon and Job (peace be on them all) and the favours of Allah the Exalted that were bestowed on them.

The Promised Messiah’s (on whom be peace) writings illustrate the Quranic prayer for a righteous family and that Allah the Exalted did not just bestow the blessing of prophethood on them, rather they were also granted with righteous progeny and worldly possessions because these prophets of God sacrificed everything of theirs for the sake of God and always gave preference to spiritual matters over worldly affairs.  This blessing is bestowed only when one gives preference to God over all else:**

Such is the lofty station of prophet hood that it can only be granted by a Being that is both mighty and generous and that Being is only Allah Who can confer this bestowal on a pauper if He so wishes, regardless of the fact whether the people around him like him or not.

(Fakhruddin al-Razi.  'Tafsir e Kabir: Mafatih al-Ghayb').

* Abul Qasim al-Hussayn ibn Mufaddal ibn Muhammad al-Raghib. 'Al Mufradat fi Gharib al-Quran'.
** Hazrat Mirza Ghulam Ahmad.  21 September 1901.  'Malfuzat: ii'.  312.

The text https://www.alislam.org/urdu/sermon/FST20020517-UR.pdf
